question: What is a LAN?
choice_a: A network spanning multiple cities
choice_b: A network within a building or nearby area
choice_c: A network connecting only to the internet
explanation: A LAN is defined by locality — it covers a building or nearby area regardless of host count.
aspect_key: local area network within a building or nearby area
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What is the role of a server in a network?
choice_a: Consumes shared resources provided by other devices
choice_b: Stores only local data with no sharing
choice_c: Provides shared files/services/resources to other devices
explanation: A server's primary role is to provide shared files and services to network clients.
aspect_key: provides shared files/services/resources to other devices
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: Which device is the core switching component in a wired LAN?
choice_a: Router
choice_b: Firewall
choice_c: WAP
explanation: A switch connects multiple wired devices in a LAN and forwards frames using MAC addresses.
aspect_key: nic; twisted-pair cable; switch; wap; router; and firewall
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What does RFI stand for?
choice_a: Remote File Interface
choice_b: Radio-Frequency Interference
choice_c: Routing Framework Interface
explanation: RFI is radio-frequency interference — electromagnetic noise that degrades network signal quality.
aspect_key: radio-frequency noise impacting transmissions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: In a peer-to-peer network what roles can each device take?
choice_a: Only a client
choice_b: Only a server
choice_c: Both client and server simultaneously
explanation: In a P2P model each endpoint can act as both a client and a server without a dedicated machine.
aspect_key: endpoints can act as both client and server in smaller networks
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: Which network classification spans the largest geographic area?
choice_a: LAN
choice_b: MAN
choice_c: WAN
explanation: A WAN (Wide Area Network) spans large geographic distances connecting multiple LANs.
aspect_key: lan (local); man (metro/city); wan (large geographic span)
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: Which topology uses a single shared backbone cable that all devices connect to?
choice_a: Star
choice_b: Ring
choice_c: Bus
explanation: Bus topology uses one shared backbone cable — all devices share the same medium.
aspect_key: one shared backbone cable; all devices share the same medium
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What type of interference comes from electromagnetic sources such as motors and power lines?
choice_a: RFI
choice_b: EMI
choice_c: FCS
explanation: EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) is noise from electromagnetic sources that degrades signal quality.
aspect_key: electromagnetic noise affecting signal quality
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: Which small-office device typically combines a router firewall WAP and switch in one unit?
choice_a: NIC
choice_b: Modem
choice_c: Wireless router
explanation: A wireless router is a common composite device combining router firewall WAP and switch functionality.
aspect_key: router; firewall; wap; and switch ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: Which topology gives every node a direct connection to every other node?
choice_a: Star
choice_b: Hub-and-spoke
choice_c: Partial mesh
explanation: Full mesh topology provides a direct interconnection between every pair of nodes in the network.
aspect_key: every node can interconnect; can use internet-based links
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What is the security baseline requirement for all endpoint devices?
choice_a: Assign static IPs to every device
choice_b: Never expose endpoints directly to the internet without firewall protection
choice_c: Use only hub-and-spoke topology
explanation: Devices must always sit behind a firewall — direct exposure to the internet is a critical security risk.
aspect_key: never expose endpoints directly to the internet without firewall protection
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: What does a network administrator manage?
choice_a: Only physical cabling and hardware
choice_b: Capacity; backups; access; maintenance; and incident response/escalation
choice_c: Only wireless devices and access points
explanation: A network admin handles the full operational scope: capacity backups access control maintenance and incident escalation.
aspect_key: manages capacity; backups; access; maintenance; and incident response/escalation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What is the key structural difference between hub-and-spoke and full mesh topology?
choice_a: Hub-and-spoke uses wireless links; full mesh uses only wired
choice_b: Hub-and-spoke centralizes traffic through one node; full mesh provides direct paths between all nodes
choice_c: Hub-and-spoke is used exclusively for WANs
choice_d: Full mesh always requires dedicated circuits; hub-and-spoke uses the internet
explanation: Hub-and-spoke is a centralized partial mesh — all traffic passes through a central point. Full mesh connects every node directly.
aspect_key: centralized style of partial mesh
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What distinguishes a WAN link from a LAN connection?
choice_a: WAN uses switches; LAN uses routers
choice_b: WAN connects multiple LANs across large geographic distances using carrier circuits
choice_c: WAN always requires fiber optic cabling
choice_d: WAN devices cannot use IP addresses
explanation: A WAN link connects LANs — anything beyond the local network boundary is treated as WAN.
aspect_key: connects lans; anything outside the local network is treated as wan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: In dedicated WAN connectivity what provides the physical links between sites?
choice_a: Internet VPN tunnels
choice_b: Satellite links only
choice_c: Provider/carrier circuits
explanation: Dedicated WAN connectivity uses provider or carrier circuits to create committed short-distance links between sites.
aspect_key: provider/carrier circuits are used for short-distance dedicated links
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: Why does the dedicated-server model outperform peer-to-peer in large enterprise networks?
choice_a: Servers use faster CPUs than endpoints
choice_b: The server is optimized for network services as its primary task providing reliability and scale
choice_c: Servers do not require IP addresses
choice_d: Peer-to-peer networks have a hard limit of 5 devices
explanation: A dedicated server focuses exclusively on service delivery making it far more reliable and scalable than P2P.
aspect_key: server is optimized for network services as its primary task
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: What is the primary purpose of a client-to-site VPN?
choice_a: Connecting two office buildings together
choice_b: Giving a remote client access into internal network resources
choice_c: Replacing the need for a perimeter firewall
choice_d: Routing traffic between VLANs on a switch
explanation: A client-to-site VPN provides a secure tunnel for remote users to access internal resources over the internet.
aspect_key: remote client access into internal network resources
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What causes fiber degradation in optical networking?
choice_a: EMI from nearby power lines
choice_b: Optical medium quality declining with distance or physical damage to the cable
choice_c: RFI from wireless access points
explanation: Fiber degradation occurs when the optical medium quality declines due to distance bends or physical damage.
aspect_key: optical medium quality can decline with distance/conditions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: In which topology do all devices connect to a single central device such as a switch?
choice_a: Bus
choice_b: Star
choice_c: Ring
explanation: Star topology has all devices connect to one central node — typically a switch — rather than to each other directly.
aspect_key: endpoints connect to a central device
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: In which topology do devices connect in a closed loop with data passing from device to device around the ring?
choice_a: Star
choice_b: Bus
choice_c: Mesh
explanation: Ring topology forms a closed loop — each device connects to exactly two neighbors and data travels around the ring.
aspect_key: devices form a closed loop
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What term describes any addressable device that participates in a network?
choice_a: Switch port
choice_b: IP packet
choice_c: Network node
explanation: A network node is any addressable or participating device in the network — from a PC to a printer to a router.
aspect_key: any addressable/participating device in the network
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What is the primary role of a client device in a network?
choice_a: Provide shared files and services to other devices
choice_b: Route packets between different network segments
choice_c: Consume shared resources provided by servers
choice_d: Manage firewall and access control policies
explanation: A client consumes resources such as files; applications; and printers that servers provide on the network.
aspect_key: consumes shared resources provided by servers
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: What does it mean for a network device to be described as online?
choice_a: The device is powered off and disconnected from the network
choice_b: The device is reachable and active on the network
choice_c: The device has a public IP address only
choice_d: The device is in a scheduled maintenance window
explanation: Online means the device is reachable and operational on the network; offline means it is unavailable.
aspect_key: online means reachable on network; offline means unavailable due to power; link; or config state
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What defines a local resource in networking?
choice_a: A file hosted on a cloud storage service
choice_b: A resource accessible only via VPN tunnel
choice_c: A resource stored and accessed directly on your own device
choice_d: Any shared resource reachable from the LAN
explanation: A local resource resides on your own device; a remote resource lives on another networked system.
aspect_key: local is on your device; remote resides on another networked system
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What is the minimum requirement to form a network?
choice_a: At least one switch and two routers
choice_b: A server providing services to at least five clients
choice_c: Two or more devices connected to exchange information
choice_d: A firewall and a dedicated WAN link
explanation: A network is simply two or more devices connected (wired or wireless) to exchange information.
aspect_key: two or more devices connected (wired or wireless) to exchange information
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: What is a WAN link?
choice_a: A cable connecting two devices in the same room
choice_b: A connection that links LANs across large geographic distances
choice_c: A wireless connection inside a single building
choice_d: The cable between a switch port and a NIC
explanation: A WAN link connects LANs — anything outside the local network boundary is treated as WAN.
aspect_key: connects lans; anything outside the local network is treated as wan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What does the shared internet model allow multiple internal devices to do?
choice_a: Each device requires its own dedicated WAN circuit
choice_b: Multiple internal devices share one router uplink to access the internet
choice_c: Devices exchange internet routing tables with each other directly
choice_d: All devices receive separate public IPs from the ISP
explanation: In a shared internet model one router uplink provides internet access to all devices on the internal LAN.
aspect_key: one router uplink can provide internet access to multiple internal devices
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: Which topology style routes all traffic through a single central node to reach any other node?
choice_a: Full mesh
choice_b: Ring
choice_c: Hub-and-spoke
explanation: Hub-and-spoke centralizes all connections — branches link only to the hub with no direct spoke-to-spoke paths.
aspect_key: centralized style of partial mesh
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What distinguishes a partial mesh from a full mesh topology?
choice_a: Partial mesh connects only two nodes; full mesh connects three or more
choice_b: In partial mesh only some nodes have redundant paths while full mesh interconnects every node
choice_c: Partial mesh uses only wireless links
choice_d: Partial mesh requires a central hub for all traffic
explanation: Partial mesh gives selected nodes redundant paths for resilience but not every pair of nodes has a direct link.
aspect_key: some nodes have redundant paths; not all
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What characterizes a mesh topology in general?
choice_a: All devices share a single backbone cable
choice_b: All devices form a closed loop
choice_c: Multiple interconnections exist between nodes providing redundant paths
choice_d: All devices connect to one central switch
explanation: Mesh topology creates multiple interconnections between nodes so data can travel via alternative paths if one link fails.
aspect_key: multiple interconnections between nodes
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What type of WAN link uses a provider or carrier circuit for a committed short-distance connection between sites?
choice_a: Internet VPN tunnel
choice_b: Satellite broadband link
choice_c: Provider/carrier dedicated circuit
explanation: Dedicated WAN links use carrier circuits that provide committed bandwidth — unlike shared internet connections.
aspect_key: provider/carrier circuits are used for short-distance dedicated links
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: What does a client-to-site VPN provide to a remote worker?
choice_a: A fiber uplink directly to the office building
choice_b: Encrypted access to internal network resources from a remote location over the internet
choice_c: A public IP address routed directly to the remote laptop
choice_d: Automatic DNS resolution for all internal hostnames
explanation: A client-to-site VPN creates an encrypted tunnel so remote users can securely reach internal resources.
aspect_key: remote client access into internal network resources
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What general term describes any signal disturbance that degrades communication quality on a network?
choice_a: Encapsulation
choice_b: Fragmentation
choice_c: Interference/noise
explanation: Interference or noise is any signal disturbance — electrical; radio; or environmental — that degrades network communication quality.
aspect_key: signal disturbance that degrades communication
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What causes optical signal degradation in a fiber cable?
choice_a: High voltage from adjacent copper cabling
choice_b: Radio frequency emissions from nearby wireless access points
choice_c: Optical medium quality declining due to physical bends; damage; or excessive distance
choice_d: Switching loops in the connected Layer 2 network
explanation: Fiber degradation occurs when the optical signal weakens due to bends; excessive distance; or physical damage to the cable.
aspect_key: optical medium quality can decline with distance/conditions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: In network operations what does it mean when a device or link is described as down?
choice_a: The device is physically rack-mounted but not yet configured
choice_b: The device is connected and actively forwarding traffic
choice_c: The link or device is non-operational due to failure; maintenance; or power loss
choice_d: The device has an expired DHCP lease
explanation: Down means off; failed; or under maintenance — the device or link is not passing traffic.
aspect_key: up means operational; down means off; failed; or under maintenance
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md

question: What is the primary value that networking provides to an organization?
choice_a: Eliminating the need for IP addressing schemes
choice_b: Sharing internet access; files; resources; programs; and user communication channels
choice_c: Reducing the physical count of servers in a data center
choice_d: Preventing all unauthorized access to company systems
explanation: The core value of a network is enabling sharing — internet; files; printers; storage; applications; and communication.
aspect_key: sharing internet access; files; resources; programs; and user communication channels
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: What does the shared resource model enable in a networked environment?
choice_a: Each user has a private printer and personal storage with no sharing
choice_b: Printers and storage are centralized and accessible by multiple users simultaneously
choice_c: Resources are distributed to every device to eliminate a single point of failure
choice_d: Only servers can access shared resources on behalf of clients
explanation: In the shared resource model centralized devices like printers and file servers serve many users at once.
aspect_key: printers and storage can be centralized and accessed by multiple users
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 1 Network Basics/Book 1 Chapter 1 - Network Basics.md

question: How does joining a shared network environment affect a user's endpoint independence?
choice_a: Users gain complete control over all shared resources and can set their own policies
choice_b: Users lose full endpoint independence as network policies; sharing; and admin controls govern behavior
choice_c: Endpoints become completely isolated from one another by default
choice_d: Users can bypass all security controls through the shared network
explanation: In shared environments network policies; centralized administration; and shared access all constrain what individual users can do independently.
aspect_key: users lose full endpoint independence in shared environments
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 1/Week 1 Notes.md
